title: 4 Establishing Policies for Canadian Dollar Money Orders
body_text:
4	Establishing Policies for Canadian Dollar Money Orders
4.1	Overview of Policies for Canadian Dollar Money Orders
Credit unions must comply with legal regulations that include but arenât limited to identification requirements, record keeping requirements, FINTRAC reporting requirements, and unclaimed property requirements. Failure to comply can result in significant fines and/or imprisonment. To ensure regulatory compliance and mitigate risk, credit unions must establish policies on ordering, selling, and maintaining an inventory of Canadian dollar Money Orders. Ensure that all staff and directors receive training appropriate to their roles both when they begin in a role and on a periodic basis thereafter (e.g., at least annually).
4.2	Policy on Setting up Canadian Dollar Money Order Services
Establish a policy to determine whether Money Orders will be drawn on a centralized account for all branches (recommended) or on a different account for each branch. The account number the credit union selects will be MICR encoded on the Money Orders.
Ensure that the account(s) is also opened on your banking system to facilitate clearing and settlement.
4.3	Policy on Complying with PCMLTFA
The credit union must establish a policy on training staff who issue and redeem Money Orders to comply with
PCMLTFA
and
PCMLTF Regulations.
Contact your AML Compliance Officer or FINTRAC for information. Financial institutions that subscribe to the Operations Manual Program can also refer to the PCMLTFA Compliance Part.
4.4	Policy on Handling Unclaimed Money Orders
Legislation in BC and Ontario governs how credit unions handle unclaimed property. Credit unions that subscribe to the Operations Manual Program can refer to the Dormant Accounts and Property Part (for BC credit unions) or the Inactive Accounts and Unclaimed Deposits Part (for Ontario credit unions). These Parts describe legal considerations and policies regarding unclaimed property.
For example, it is recommended that the credit union develop policies to restrict access to unclaimed property to a few senior staff members. Since unclaimed property may be susceptible to employee fraud during an extended period of dormancy, segregating duties and restricting access to the property are key internal controls. The credit union can also establish internal control policies and procedures for monitoring activity on unclaimed property.
4.5	Policy on Replacing or Refunding Money Orders
Credit unions must establish a policy on replacing lost, stolen, or destroyed Canadian dollar Money Orders. Since these items are drawn on the credit union, the risk of loss is greater than that for negotiable instruments issued by another entity and sold by the credit union. Canadian dollar Money Orders are like cash. The credit union canât refuse to honour Money Orders that are presented for payment or processed through the Clearing
even if the credit union has replaced or refunded the original item.
Therefore, Central 1 recommends that credit unions donât replace or refund lost, stolen, or destroyed Canadian dollar Money Orders.
However, the credit union can choose to replace or refund lost, stolen, or destroyed Money Orders on a case-by-case basis. Ensure that the member (the purchaser) signs an indemnity agreement. The Indemnity Agreement for Lost, Stolen, or Destroyed Documents, Form 1245, is available to credit unions that subscribe to the Operations Manual Program. Donât replace or refund Money Orders for non-members. See Section 7.3, Replacing or Refunding Canadian Dollar Money Orders.
Ensure that staff are trained in the proper use of indemnity agreements. Indemnity agreements should never be used as an automatic solution to a lost document (e.g. Money Order). First, the credit union isnât obligated to replace any documents. Second, the decision to replace a document such as an official cheque or a Money Order should be based on the credibility of the indemnitor and the likelihood of collection from the indemnitor if the lost, stolen, or destroyed Money Order is cashed. It is a credit union decision to replace a Money Order. An indemnity agreement shouldnât be relied on to protect the credit union from risk of loss unless collateral is provided. Consider the following factors when deciding if a Money Order should be replaced:
Is the indemnitor a member?
Does the member have assets and income?
Does he or she have stable residence and employment in the community?
Can or will the indemnitor provide collateral?
Related Information
Section 7.3
, Replacing or Refunding Canadian Dollar Money Orders
Indemnity Agreement for Lost, Stolen, or Destroyed Documents,
Form 1245
(available to subscribers of the Operations Manual Program)
4.6	Policy on Obtaining Payment for Money Orders
Establish a policy to ensure that the funds received for payment of a Money Order are guaranteed or cleared before a Money Order is given to the purchaser.
Money Orders are like cash. They must be honoured when they are presented for payment or cashed and settled through the Clearing
even if payment of the Money Order is dishonoured
.
4.7	Policy on Selling Money Orders to Non-Members
If the credit union chooses to sell Money Orders to non-members, it must establish a policy to limit the risk of such transactions. The credit union is less likely to encounter suspicious transactions when dealing with members given that the credit union has conducted its due diligence and has developed an ongoing relationship with the individual. Therefore, it is recommended that credit unions donât sell Money Orders to non-members.
If a credit union chooses to sell Money Orders of $3,000 (Canadian equivalent) or more to a non-member, it must ascertain the identity of the individual and keep a record of the individualâs name and address, the date of the transaction, and the amount received. These records also must indicate whether the amount was received in cash, cheques, travelers cheques, Money Orders, or other similar negotiable instruments. This identification requirement also applies if two or more Money Orders totaling $3,000 (Canadian Equivalent) or more are issued.
The risk of dealing with non-members is greater than dealing with members. As such, the credit union can establish a policy to ascertain the identity of non-members regardless of the amount of the Money Order. The financial institution can choose to keep a record of all Money Orders for non-members regardless of the amount.
4.8	Policy on Internal Controls for Money Orders
Credit unions are responsible for security practices and internal controls. Operations staff and external and internal auditors must establish internal controls for ordering, selling, and maintaining an inventory of Money Orders.
Such policies can include but arenât limited to:
Receiving and verifying Money Order shipments in joint custody
Maintaining the vault register in joint custody
Monitoring the inventory of blank Money Orders to ensure that the number of physical items corresponds to the vault register
Maintaining a log of Money Orders issued to each branch (if applicable)
Using an imprinter rather than handwriting to complete Money Orders
Reconciling cleared Money Orders daily to ensure that the file of issued but uncashed Money Orders is current
END
